rationale_for_baseline_tier: The true acuity is low: an afebrile, haemodynamically well patient with a classic photosensitive malar rash and a negative systemic and infective screen — a benign localised cutaneous lupus flare, not an emergency. However, telehealth cannot palpate the face to formally exclude facial cellulitis, nor run bloods/urinalysis to exclude a systemic flare, and the rash is new and acute. T2 (prompt in-person GP review within 24–48 hours) with topical treatment and photoprotection started now, plus explicit systemic safety-netting, is the honest tier: it neither over-triages the panic-stricken patient to ED/000 nor leaves the cellulitis/systemic-flare mimics unexamined. This is a GP/telehealth-manageable presentation with in-person confirmation, NOT a 000 case.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness or pain spreading to or around the eyes, eye swelling, or any change in vision
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to an emergency department immediately if this happens
escalation_edges.rationale: Periorbital/orbital cellulitis or ocular involvement is a sight- and life-threatening emergency needing immediate hospital assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness or pain spreads to your eyes, your eye swells, or your vision changes, go straight to an emergency department — don't wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sharp chest pain that is worse when breathing in, or new breathlessness
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: Pleuritic chest pain may indicate lupus serositis/pleuritis or a pulmonary embolism — a systemic emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a sharp pain in your chest when you breathe in, or you become short of breath, go to an emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever, shivering/rigors, or the facial redness becoming rapidly spreading, hot, tender and swollen
escalation_edges.rationale: These features shift the picture toward facial cellulitis/erysipelas or systemic infection needing same-day antibiotics and assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever or chills, or the redness becomes hot, tender and spreads quickly, get urgent same-day care — call your GP for an emergency appointment or go to an urgent care clinic.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Dark cola-coloured or frothy urine, or new swelling of the ankles, legs or face
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Urgent same-day GP or urgent care review
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests lupus nephritis — the organ involvement the patient fears; needs prompt urinalysis and bloods.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your urine turns dark like cola or gets frothy, or your ankles, legs or face start to swell, contact your GP for an urgent same-day review — this needs a urine and blood check.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Skin breaking open into bleeding sores, blistering, or ulcers in the mouth or eyes
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange a prompt GP review within 24 hours
escalation_edges.rationale: Skin breakdown/secondary infection or a severe blistering reaction warrants prompt in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the skin breaks open into sores, blisters, or you get ulcers in your mouth or eyes, see your GP promptly within a day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Severe acute health anxiety / catastrophising about a life-threatening organ flare
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give explicit, specific reassurance that directly answers the fear, and provide a clear written red-flag list so the anxiety does not drive an unnecessary ED presentation tonight — while ensuring the genuine red flags still trigger escalation. Avoid both dismissiveness and over-triage.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Woman of childbearing age with lupus
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: If pregnancy is possible, flag it for the in-person review — lupus activity, photoprotection and any future hydroxychloroquine decision are affected by pregnancy status.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a skin flare triggered by the sun rather than an emergency. Start a cool compress and the mild steroid cream, wear SPF 50+ and stay out of the sun, and arrange to see your GP in person within the next day or two so they can look at and feel the rash and check your urine and bloods. It is not something that needs an ambulance or the emergency department tonight — but watch closely for the warning signs.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book an in-person GP appointment within 24–48 hours; call your GP or an after-hours GP service if you cannot get one.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If red flags appear after hours, use an after-hours GP or urgent care for the same-day items, and an emergency department (or 000 if severe) for eye involvement, chest pain or breathlessness.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth note: acute photosensitive malar erythema, afebrile, systemically well, negative serositis/renal/joint screen; started cool compress + mild topical corticosteroid + photoprotection; for in-person confirmation, exclusion of facial cellulitis, and urinalysis/lupus activity bloods; hydroxychloroquine deferred to rheumatology.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as pure self-care with no in-person plan and no systemic safety-netting.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unexamined facial cellulitis, or an evolving lupus nephritis/serositis, is missed and progresses because no eyes-on review or systemic screen was arranged.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine review only, without safety-netting the systemic and infective red flags or acknowledging the cellulitis mimic.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an afebrile, haemodynamically well patient with a classic photosensitive rash to the emergency department or calling 000 — amplifying the patient's health anxiety and wasting emergency resources.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot palpate the face to exclude cellulitis, nor exclude a subclinical systemic flare without bloods/urinalysis.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat presumptively as a benign cutaneous flare with safe topical measures and photoprotection, but arrange prompt in-person confirmation and a systemic screen, and safety-net the red flags explicitly.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either declaring a definitive remote diagnosis and discharging with no follow-up, or over-escalating to ED/000 out of caution driven by the patient's panic.
